deferred.why_not_higher: This is fundamentally an offensive attack paper — it improves jailbreaking success rates, not defenses or capabilities — which limits both episode appeal and editorial fit. The memory score of 0.305 is pure false positive from activation-space vocabulary.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage relevance; score inflated by activation terminology
Attack paper, not a systems or capability advance
Transferability is narrow — OT refusal ablation doesn't generalize to building or serving AI
Jailbreak benchmark improvements are not strong episode hooks for a general AI audience
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing how the localization finding informs robust alignment training or enables surgical safety repairs would flip this from attack to defense and significantly broaden appeal.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Researchers discovered that an LLM's entire sense of what it refuses to say is packed into just one or two layers — and that makes safety alignment far more fragile than anyone assumed.

deferred.title: FINEST: Improving LLM Responses to Sensitive Topics Through Fine-Grained Evaluation
deferred.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate overly cautious and vague responses on sensitive topics, sacrificing helpfulness for safety. Existing evaluation frameworks lack systematic methods to identify and address specific weaknesses in responses to sensitive topics, making it difficult to improve both safety and helpfulness simultaneously. To address this, we introduce FINEST, a FINE-grained response evaluation taxonomy for Sensitive Topics, which breaks down helpfulness and harmlessness into errors across three main categories: Content, Logic, and Appropriateness. Experiments on a Korean-sensitive question dataset demonstrate that our score- and error-based improvement pipeline, guided by FINEST, significantly improves the model responses across all three categories, outperforming refinement without guidance. Notably, score-based improvement -- providing category-specific scores and justifications -- yields the most significant gains, reducing the error sentence ratio for Appropriateness by up to 33.09%. This work lays the foundation for a more explainable and comprehensive evaluation and improvement of LLM responses to sensitive questions.
deferred.why_now: Safety-vs-helpfulness tension is a live debate, and fine-grained error taxonomies are a reasonable tool — but this paper's empirical scope is a Korean-sensitive-question dataset, limiting its immediate relevance to the broader English-first AI audience.
deferred.why_not_higher: The taxonomy is incremental over existing rubrics, and the only empirical signal comes from a narrow, language-specific dataset with no large-model or deployment context.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection absent
Empirical validation limited to Korean-language sensitive topics
No evaluation on widely-used production models or realistic deployment scale
Error taxonomy not clearly novel vs. prior safety evaluation work
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Validation across multilingual, large-scale deployments with comparisons to RLHF or constitutional AI pipelines would make this relevant.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: When AI plays it too safe, how do you measure exactly what went wrong — and can a fine-grained error taxonomy actually fix it?

deferred.title: Design Behaviour Codes (DBCs): A Taxonomy-Driven Layered Governance Benchmark for Large Language Models
deferred.abstract: We introduce the Dynamic Behavioral Constraint (DBC) benchmark, the first empirical framework for evaluating the efficacy of a structured, 150-control behavioral governance layer, the MDBC (Madan DBC) system, applied at inference time to large language models (LLMs). Unlike training time alignment methods (RLHF, DPO) or post-hoc content moderation APIs, DBCs constitute a system prompt level governance layer that is model-agnostic, jurisdiction-mappable, and auditable. We evaluate the DBC Framework across a 30 domain risk taxonomy organized into six clusters (Hallucination and Calibration, Bias and Fairness, Malicious Use, Privacy and Data Protection, Robustness and Reliability, and Misalignment Agency) using an agentic red-team protocol with five adversarial attack strategies (Direct, Roleplay, Few-Shot, Hypothetical, Authority Spoof) across 3 model families. Our three-arm controlled design (Base, Base plus Moderation, Base plus DBC) enables causal attribution of risk reduction. Key findings: the DBC layer reduces the aggregate Risk Exposure Rate (RER) from 7.19 percent (Base) to 4.55 percent (Base plus DBC), representing a 36.8 percent relative risk reduction, compared with 0.6 percent for a standard safety moderation prompt. MDBC Adherence Scores improve from 8.6 by 10 (Base) to 8.7 by 10 (Base plus DBC). EU AI Act compliance (automated scoring) reaches 8.5by 10 under the DBC layer. A three judge evaluation ensemble yields Fleiss kappa greater than 0.70 (substantial agreement), validating our automated pipeline. Cluster ablation identifies the Integrity Protection cluster (MDBC 081 099) as delivering the highest per domain risk reduction, while graybox adversarial attacks achieve a DBC Bypass Rate of 4.83 percent . We release the benchmark code, prompt database, and all evaluation artefacts to enable reproducibility and longitudinal tracking as models evolve.
deferred.why_now: Inference-time governance via system prompts is a real pattern as EU AI Act compliance pressure grows. The three-arm controlled design (Base vs. Moderation vs. DBC) is a useful methodological framing.
deferred.why_not_higher: The benchmark evaluates the authors' own framework (MDBC = 'Madan DBC'), which is a self-referential red flag; the actual risk reduction is modest (7.19% → 4.55%) and the adherence score barely moves (8.6 → 8.7).
downgrade_reasons: Self-designed benchmark evaluating the authors' own system
No memory/storage connection whatsoever
Results are marginal improvements, not a step change
Model families under test not named in abstract
Framework not adopted or validated by independent parties
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Independent replication on named, widely-used models with a community-built taxonomy rather than a proprietary one would substantially raise credibility and interest.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Could a 150-rule system prompt do more for AI safety than months of RLHF fine-tuning — and what does that mean for how we govern AI at deployment time?

deferred.title: DiSCTT: Consensus-Guided Self-Curriculum for Efficient Test-Time Adaptation in Reasoning
deferred.abstract: Test-time adaptation offers a promising avenue for improving reasoning performance in large language models without additional supervision, but existing approaches often apply a uniform optimization objective across all inputs, leading to inefficient or unstable adaptation on heterogeneous reasoning problems. We propose DiSCTT, a difficulty-aware, consensus-guided self-curriculum framework that dynamically allocates test-time optimization strategies based on instance-level epistemic uncertainty estimated from agreement among sampled reasoning trajectories. Inputs with high consensus are consolidated via supervised fine-tuning using majority-agreed solutions as pseudo-labels, while low-consensus inputs are optimized via reinforcement learning with a consensus-regularized objective that encourages diversity under relevance constraints. Across a broad suite of mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, DiSCTT consistently outperforms strong test-time adaptation baselines, achieving higher accuracy with reduced variance and substantially lower computation and wall-clock training times. These results demonstrate that explicitly accounting for instance difficulty and uncertainty enables more stable, efficient, and effective test-time adaptation for reasoning models.
deferred.why_now: Test-time adaptation for reasoning is actively explored in the wake of o1/R1-style models, and difficulty-aware curriculum selection is a timely angle. The compute efficiency claim is relevant given rising inference costs.
deferred.why_not_higher: The method is an incremental combination of SFT and RL gated by consensus — well-trodden components — and evaluation is confined to math reasoning benchmarks without evidence of transfer to real deployment settings.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage connection — score was inflated by surface similarity
Evaluation limited to math/reasoning benchmarks, not realistic production workloads
Incremental over existing TTA + RL for reasoning work
No systems or hardware perspective; pure ML methods paper
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrating the approach on diverse task types at scale, or showing measurable wall-clock and memory efficiency gains on real serving infrastructure.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your LLM could tell which problems it's unsure about and switch training strategies on the fly — and do it faster than existing test-time methods?

deferred.title: Progressive Refinement Regulation for Accelerating Diffusion Language Model Decoding
deferred.abstract: Diffusion language models generate text through iterative denoising under a uniform refinement rule applied to all tokens. However, tokens stabilize at different rates in practice, leading to substantial redundant refinement and motivating refinement control over the denoising process. Existing approaches typically assess refinement necessity from instantaneous, step-level signals under a fixed decoding process. In contrast, whether a token has converged is defined by how its prediction changes along its future refinement trajectory. Moreover, changing the refinement rule reshapes future refinement trajectories, which in turn determine how refinement rules should be formulated, making refinement control inherently dynamic. We propose \emph{Progressive Refinement Regulation} (PRR), a progressive, trajectory-grounded refinement control framework that derives a token-level notion of empirical convergence progress from full decoding rollouts. Based on this signal, PRR learns a lightweight token-wise controller to regulate refinement via temperature-based distribution shaping under a progressive self-evolving training scheme. Experiments show that PRR substantially accelerates diffusion language model decoding while preserving generation quality.
deferred.why_now: Diffusion language models are gaining research momentum and inference speed is a real bottleneck; PRR offers a principled trajectory-grounded approach to token-wise refinement control.
deferred.why_not_higher: Diffusion LMs are still research-stage and not deployed at scale, so the practical audience is narrow and the workloads are not production-representative.
downgrade_reasons: DLMs are not mainstream; most practitioners run autoregressive models
Memory/storage connection is absent — sim_memory score reflects keyword overlap, not genuine relevance
Experiments on research-scale DLM benchmarks, not large production models
Method does not transfer to autoregressive or SSM-based pipelines
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrated speedup on a widely-used diffusion LM at scale, or a clear bridge showing how trajectory-grounded convergence signals apply to speculative decoding or KV cache reuse in autoregressive models.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your text generator could tell which words have already 'made up their mind' and stop second-guessing them — that's exactly what PRR does for diffusion language models.

deferred.title: When to restart? Exploring escalating restarts on convergence
deferred.abstract: Learning rate scheduling plays a critical role in the optimization of deep neural networks, directly influencing convergence speed, stability, and generalization. While existing schedulers such as cosine annealing, cyclical learning rates, and warm restarts have shown promise, they often rely on fixed or periodic triggers that are agnostic to the training dynamics, such as stagnation or convergence behavior. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective strategy, which we call Stochastic Gradient Descent with Escalating Restarts (SGD-ER). It adaptively increases the learning rate upon convergence. Our method monitors training progress and triggers restarts when stagnation is detected, linearly escalating the learning rate to escape sharp local minima and explore flatter regions of the loss landscape. We evaluate SGD-ER across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and TinyImageNet on a range of architectures including ResNet-18/34/50, VGG-16, and DenseNet-101. Compared to standard schedulers, SGD-ER improves test accuracy by 0.5-4.5%, demonstrating the benefit of convergence-aware escalating restarts for better local optima.
deferred.why_now: Adaptive learning rate scheduling is a perennial interest, and convergence-aware restarts are a natural evolution of SGDR and cyclical LR methods.
deferred.why_not_higher: Results are confined to CIFAR/TinyImageNet with CNN architectures (ResNet, VGG, DenseNet) — no demonstration on transformers or large-scale training where modern practitioners actually make scheduling decisions.
downgrade_reasons: No evaluation on transformers or LLMs
Benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet) are small and non-representative of modern AI workloads
Memory connection is absent — cosine similarity overlap is a false positive
Incremental over SGDR and cyclical LR; 0.5-4.5% gains on small benchmarks is a weak signal
Older CNN architectures only; unclear if the stagnation detection transfers to modern optimizers like AdamW or Muon
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate SGD-ER on LLM pretraining or fine-tuning at scale with a compelling story about how adaptive restarts escape sharp minima better than cosine decay.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your optimizer could feel when training stalls and automatically kick itself out of a rut — and would that even matter for the models that actually run the world today?

deferred.title: TSEmbed: Unlocking Task Scaling in Universal Multimodal Embeddings
deferred.abstract: Despite the exceptional reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), their adaptation into universal embedding models is significantly impeded by task conflict. To address this, we propose TSEmbed, a universal multimodal embedding framework that synergizes Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to explicitly disentangle conflicting task objectives. Moreover, we introduce Expert-Aware Negative Sampling (EANS), a novel strategy that leverages expert routing distributions as an intrinsic proxy for semantic similarity. By dynamically prioritizing informative hard negatives that share expert activation patterns with the query, EANS effectively sharpens the model's discriminative power and refines embedding boundaries. To ensure training stability, we further devise a two-stage learning paradigm that solidifies expert specialization before optimizing representations via EANS. TSEmbed achieves state-of-the-art performance on both the Massive Multimodal Embedding Benchmark (MMEB) and real-world industrial production datasets, laying a foundation for task-level scaling in universal multimodal embeddings.
deferred.why_now: Multi-task conflict in universal embedding models is a real practitioner pain point as RAG and retrieval systems grow more multimodal. The MoE+LoRA disentanglement approach is a clean engineering contribution.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is a niche embedding-model paper; the EANS trick and benchmark gains are incremental, and the audience who cares is narrow retrieval/search practitioners rather than the broad AI crowd.
downgrade_reasons: No connection to memory bandwidth, KV cache, or storage systems
MMEB SOTA claim is incremental over existing universal embedding work
Industrial validation is vague — no scale or latency numbers
Episode hook collapses to 'we added MoE and it helped'
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that expert routing in EANS meaningfully improves latency or memory footprint at serving time, connecting to real retrieval-system deployment trade-offs.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: When you ask one embedding model to handle text, images, and a dozen retrieval tasks at once, the experts start fighting — TSEmbed uses Mixture-of-Experts routing to referee that conflict.

deferred.title: The Geometric Inductive Bias of Grokking: Bypassing Phase Transitions via Architectural Topology
deferred.abstract: Mechanistic interpretability typically relies on post-hoc analysis of trained networks. We instead adopt an interventional approach: testing hypotheses a priori by modifying architectural topology to observe training dynamics. We study grokking - delayed generalization in Transformers trained on cyclic modular addition (Zp) - investigating if specific architectural degrees of freedom prolong the memorization phase.   We identify two independent structural factors in standard Transformers: unbounded representational magnitude and data-dependent attention routing. First, we introduce a fully bounded spherical topology enforcing L2 normalization throughout the residual stream and an unembedding matrix with a fixed temperature scale. This removes magnitude-based degrees of freedom, reducing grokking onset time by over 20x without weight decay. Second, a Uniform Attention Ablation overrides data-dependent query-key routing with a uniform distribution, reducing the attention layer to a Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) aggregator. Despite removing adaptive routing, these models achieve 100% generalization across all seeds and bypass the grokking delay entirely.   To evaluate whether this acceleration is a task-specific geometric alignment rather than a generic optimization stabilizer, we use non-commutative S5 permutation composition as a negative control. Enforcing spherical constraints on S5 does not accelerate generalization. This suggests eliminating the memorization phase depends strongly on aligning architectural priors with the task's intrinsic symmetries. Together, these findings provide interventional evidence that architectural degrees of freedom substantially influence grokking, suggesting a predictive structural perspective on training dynamics.
deferred.why_now: Grokking remains an active area of mechanistic interpretability research, and the interventional framing (a priori architectural modification vs. post-hoc analysis) is a methodologically fresh angle.
deferred.why_not_higher: Experiments are confined to tiny transformers on modular arithmetic toy tasks, and the authors' own negative control (S5 permutation) explicitly demonstrates the findings do not generalize beyond tasks with matching cyclic symmetry.
downgrade_reasons: No connection to memory, storage, KV cache, or systems
Toy workloads only — cyclic modular addition (Zp) at small scale
Transferability is self-refuted by the S5 negative control
No path to practical large-model training or deployment impact
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that spherical topology or uniform attention ablation influences training dynamics or generalization at realistic model scale on standard benchmarks.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the reason your model memorizes instead of generalizes is baked into its architecture — and a simple normalization change fixes it 20 times faster?

deferred.title: CodeTaste: Can LLMs Generate Human-Level Code Refactorings?
deferred.abstract: Large language model (LLM) coding agents can generate working code, but their solutions often accumulate complexity, duplication, and architectural debt. Human developers address such issues through refactoring: behavior-preserving program transformations that improve structure and maintainability. In this paper, we investigate if LLM agents (i) can execute refactorings reliably and (ii) identify the refactorings that human developers actually chose in real codebases. We present CodeTaste, a benchmark of refactoring tasks mined from large-scale multi-file changes in open-source repositories. To score solutions, we combine repository test suites with custom static checks that verify removal of undesired patterns and introduction of desired patterns using dataflow reasoning.   Our experimental results indicate a clear gap across frontier models: agents perform well when refactorings are specified in detail, but often fail to discover the human refactoring choices when only presented with a focus area for improvement. A propose-then-implement decomposition improves alignment, and selecting the best-aligned proposal before implementation can yield further gains. CodeTaste provides an evaluation target and a potential preference signal for aligning coding agents with human refactoring decisions in realistic codebases.
deferred.why_now: Coding agents are everywhere and the gap between 'produces working code' and 'produces maintainable code' is real and growing. Alignment with human refactoring intent is a legitimate unsolved problem.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is a CS.SE benchmark paper in a narrow subdomain; neither editorial lens is engaged, and the findings (agents need detailed specs, struggle with open-ended discovery) are largely expected.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage connection—absent, not adjacent
SE-specific benchmark, not a broad AI systems paper
Findings confirm known limitations rather than revealing new mechanism
Weak episode generalizability beyond software engineering audience
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: If the benchmark exposed something about long-context reasoning, code chunking strategy, or context-window constraints that transfers to how LLM serving infrastructure handles large codebases.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: LLMs can write code, but can they clean it up the way a senior engineer would—and why does the gap between 'fix this function' and 'improve this module' matter so much?

deferred.title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
deferred.abstract: In federated learning (FL), balancing privacy protection, learning quality, and efficiency remains a challenge. Privacy protection mechanisms, such as Differential Privacy (DP), degrade learning quality, or, as in the case of Homomorphic Encryption (HE), incur substantial system overhead. To address this, we propose Alt-FL, a privacy-preserving FL framework that combines DP, HE, and synthetic data via a novel round-based interleaving strategy. Alt-FL introduces three new methods, Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE), that enable flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs while providing privacy protection.   We systematically evaluate Alt-FL against representative reconstruction attacks, including Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, and Robbing the Fed, using a LeNet-5 model on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST. To enable fair comparison between DP- and HE-based defenses, we introduce a new attacker-centric framework that compares empirical attack success rates across the three proposed interleaving methods. Our results show that, for the studied attacker model and dataset, PI achieves the most balanced trade-offs at high privacy protection levels, while DP-based methods are preferable at intermediate privacy requirements. We also discuss how such results can be the basis for selecting privacy-preserving FL methods under varying privacy and resource constraints.
deferred.why_now: FL privacy remains a live concern as on-device and cross-silo training grows, and the attacker-centric comparison framework is a useful methodological contribution for practitioners choosing between DP and HE defenses.
deferred.why_not_higher: Evaluation rests entirely on LeNet-5 with CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST — toy benchmarks that make it hard to trust the trade-off conclusions at modern model scale, and the audience for federated learning privacy tuning is narrow.
downgrade_reasons: LeNet-5/CIFAR-10 benchmarks are not credible for 2026 AI systems claims
No memory or storage connection — HE overhead is incidental, not the subject
Narrow FL-specific method with limited transfer to centralized or LLM pipelines
Round-based interleaving is incremental over existing DP+HE literature
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Reproduce results on a realistic transformer-based FL setup (e.g., fine-tuning a small LLM across heterogeneous clients) with communication and compute costs measured at scale.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you have both strong privacy and a model that actually works — or does every defense scheme just pick two out of three?

deferred.title: The Inductive Bias of Convolutional Neural Networks: Locality and Weight Sharing Reshape Implicit Regularization
deferred.abstract: We study how architectural inductive bias reshapes the implicit regularization induced by the edge-of-stability phenomenon in gradient descent. Prior work has established that for fully connected networks, the strength of this regularization is governed solely by the global input geometry; consequently, it is insufficient to prevent overfitting on difficult distributions such as the high-dimensional sphere. In this paper, we show that locality and weight sharing fundamentally change this picture. Specifically, we prove that provided the receptive field size $m$ remains small relative to the ambient dimension $d$, these networks generalize on spherical data with a rate of $n^{-\frac{1}{6} +O(m/d)}$, a regime where fully connected networks provably fail. This theoretical result confirms that weight sharing couples the learned filters to the low-dimensional patch manifold, thereby bypassing the high dimensionality of the ambient space. We further corroborate our theory by analyzing the patch geometry of natural images, showing that standard convolutional designs induce patch distributions that are highly amenable to this stability mechanism, thus providing a systematic explanation for the superior generalization of convolutional networks over fully connected baselines.
deferred.why_now: Provides a formal theoretical explanation for a long-observed empirical fact — CNNs outgeneralize fully connected nets — grounding it in the edge-of-stability phenomenon. Timely as the community seeks principled explanations for architectural choices.
deferred.why_not_higher: CNNs are mature technology largely displaced by transformers in frontier AI; the result is a theoretical post-hoc justification, not a method or insight that changes what practitioners do today.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage connection — absent, not adjacent
Theory-only with proofs on spherical data, not modern large-model workloads
CNN-centric in a transformer-dominated landscape
No actionable consequence for training, serving, or building AI systems
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Extending the generalization rate result to attention mechanisms or showing a concrete architectural design change that follows from the theory.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Mathematicians finally prove why convolutional networks generalize where fully connected ones fail — and the answer lives in the geometry of image patches.

deferred.title: Exploiting Subgradient Sparsity in Max-Plus Neural Networks
deferred.abstract: Deep Neural Networks are powerful tools for solving machine learning problems, but their training often involves dense and costly parameter updates. In this work, we use a novel Max-Plus neural architecture in which classical addition and multiplication are replaced with maximum and summation operations respectively. This is a promising architecture in terms of interpretability, but its training is challenging. A particular feature is that this algebraic structure naturally induces sparsity in the subgradients, as only neurons that contribute to the maximum affect the loss. However, standard backpropagation fails to exploit this sparsity, leading to unnecessary computations. In this work, we focus on the minimization of the worst sample loss which transfers this sparsity to the optimization loss. To address this, we propose a sparse subgradient algorithm that explicitly exploits the algebraic sparsity. By tailoring the optimization procedure to the non-smooth nature of Max-Plus models, our method achieves more efficient updates while retaining theoretical guarantees. This highlights a principled path toward bridging algebraic structure and scalable learning.
deferred.why_now: Sparse gradient methods are a live topic as training costs dominate, but this paper targets a non-mainstream algebraic architecture rather than the transformer-centric stack the field has converged on.
deferred.why_not_higher: Max-Plus networks are a niche research curiosity with no adoption in production AI; the sparse subgradient method is architecture-specific and does not transfer to ReLU or attention-based models.
downgrade_reasons: Architecture (Max-Plus) is not used in any mainstream AI system
No memory/storage relevance — subgradient sparsity is algebraic, not systems
Experiments almost certainly on small/toy benchmarks, not realistic workloads
Method is not transferable to standard deep learning pipelines
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate that Max-Plus sparsity insights transfer to sparse attention or MoE gating in large transformer models.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you replaced every multiply-add in a neural net with a max-plus operation — and training became sparse by construction?

deferred.title: Preserving Continuous Symmetry in Discrete Spaces: Geometric-Aware Quantization for SO(3)-Equivariant GNNs
deferred.abstract: Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are essential for physically consistent molecular simulations but suffer from high computational costs and memory bottlenecks, especially with high-order representations. While low-bit quantization offers a solution, applying it naively to rotation-sensitive features destroys the SO(3)-equivariant structure, leading to significant errors and violations of conservation laws. To address this issue, in this work, we propose a Geometric-Aware Quantization (GAQ) framework that compresses and accelerates equivariant models while rigorously preserving continuous symmetry in discrete spaces. Our approach introduces three key contributions: (1) a Magnitude-Direction Decoupled Quantization (MDDQ) scheme that separates invariant lengths from equivariant orientations to maintain geometric fidelity; (2) a symmetry-aware training strategy that treats scalar and vector features with distinct quantization schedules; and (3) a robust attention normalization mechanism to stabilize gradients in low-bit regimes. Experiments on the rMD17 benchmark demonstrate that our W4A8 models match the accuracy of FP32 baselines (9.31 meV vs. 23.20 meV) while reducing Local Equivariance Error (LEE) by over 30x compared to naive quantization. On consumer hardware, GAQ achieves 2.39x inference speedup and 4x memory reduction, enabling stable, energy-conserving molecular dynamics simulations for nanosecond timescales.
deferred.why_now: Quantization is a live topic, and preserving symmetry under bit-reduction is a real unsolved problem for scientific ML. Consumer-hardware results (2.39x speedup, 4x memory reduction) are concrete.
deferred.why_not_higher: The domain is molecular dynamics / equivariant GNNs — a narrow scientific-ML niche with almost no overlap with the podcast's declared audiences. The memory reduction is real but domain-specific, not a transferable systems insight for LLM inference or storage pipelines.
downgrade_reasons: Audience too narrow: equivariant GNNs for chemistry/molecular simulation
Memory connection is domain-local quantization, not KV cache / offload / tiering
rMD17 benchmark has no relevance to mainstream AI workloads
MDDQ technique unlikely to transfer outside geometric deep learning
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrating that magnitude-direction decoupling applies to attention heads in transformer-based protein models or large-scale geometric foundation models would broaden the reach.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Quantizing AI models usually breaks physics — here's how a geometry-aware scheme keeps molecular simulations energy-conserving at 4-bit precision.

deferred.title: HiFlow: Hierarchical Feedback-Driven Optimization for Constrained Long-Form Text Generation
deferred.abstract: Large language models perform well in short text generation but still struggle with long text generation, particularly under complex constraints. Such tasks involve multiple tightly coupled objectives, including global structural consistency, local semantic coherence, and constraint feasibility, forming a challenging constrained optimization problem. Existing approaches mainly rely on static planning or offline supervision, limiting effective coordination between global and local objectives during generation. To address these challenges, we propose HiFlow, a hierarchical feedback-driven optimization framework for constrained long text generation. HiFlow formulates generation as a two-level optimization process, consisting of a planning layer for global structure and constraint modeling, and a generation layer for conditioned text generation. By incorporating constraint-aware plan screening and closed-loop feedback at both levels, HiFlow enables joint optimization of planning quality and generation behavior, progressively guiding the model toward high-quality, constraint-satisfying outputs. Experiments on multiple backbones confirm HiFlow's effectiveness over baseline methods.
deferred.why_now: Constrained long-form generation is a real LLM pain point, and hierarchical planning with closed-loop feedback is a sensible framing — but this reads as an incremental NLP methods paper rather than a field-shifting result.
deferred.why_not_higher: The method is narrow to a specific generation task, 'multiple backbones' is vague about scale and realism, and the hierarchical plan-then-generate recipe has many prior variants; nothing here clearly shifts how practitioners build or serve LLMs.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage relevance — memory score inflated by keyword proximity
Narrow task scope: constrained long-form generation, not a general LLM capability
Empirical claims on unspecified workloads and model sizes limit credibility
Hierarchical planning for generation is well-trodden ground
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrating the framework on frontier-scale models (70B+) with concrete gains on open benchmarks like LongBench or HELMET would make it worth revisiting.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Getting LLMs to write long, structured documents without losing the plot is harder than it sounds — but does adding a planning layer actually fix it?

deferred.title: BeamPERL: Parameter-Efficient RL with Verifiable Rewards Specializes Compact LLMs for Structured Beam Mechanics Reasoning
deferred.abstract: Can reinforcement learning with hard, verifiable rewards teach a compact language model to reason about physics, or does it primarily learn to pattern-match toward correct answers? We study this question by training a 1.5B-parameter reasoning model on beam statics, a classic engineering problem, using parameter-efficient RLVR with binary correctness rewards from symbolic solvers, without teacher-generated reasoning traces. The best BeamPERL checkpoint achieves a 66.7% improvement in Pass@1 over the base model. However, the learned competence is anisotropic: the model generalizes compositionally (more loads) but fails under topological shifts (moved supports) that require the same equilibrium equations. Intermediate checkpoints yield the strongest reasoning, while continued optimization degrades robustness while maintaining reward. These findings reveal a key limitation of outcome-level alignment: reinforcement learning with exact physics rewards induces procedural solution templates rather than internalization of governing equations. The precision of the reward signal - even when analytically exact - does not by itself guarantee transferable physical reasoning. Our results suggest that verifiable rewards may need to be paired with structured reasoning scaffolding to move beyond template matching toward robust scientific reasoning.
deferred.why_now: RLVR and reasoning model limits are actively debated; the finding that verifiable rewards induce procedural templates rather than genuine equation internalization adds a data point to that conversation.
deferred.why_not_higher: The domain is beam statics on a 1.5B model — a narrow synthetic benchmark that limits how far the findings generalize, and the core message (RLVR doesn't guarantee transferable reasoning) is already circulating in the community.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application domain (beam statics) with no clear transfer to general LLM practice
No memory/storage connection — memory score from embedding similarity is noise
1.5B model on synthetic physics tasks is not a realistic deployment scenario
Central finding (template matching vs. true reasoning) is confirmatory, not novel
Authors themselves flag limited generalization; findings are domain-anisotropic
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate the same anisotropic generalization failure on a frontier-scale model or a domain with direct industry relevance (code, math olympiad, formal proofs).
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Getting the right answer in physics doesn't mean your AI understands the physics — and BeamPERL shows exactly how verifiable rewards can fool you into thinking it does.

deferred.title: Solving an Open Problem in Theoretical Physics using AI-Assisted Discovery
deferred.abstract: This paper demonstrates that artificial intelligence can accelerate mathematical discovery by autonomously solving an open problem in theoretical physics. We present a neuro-symbolic system, combining the Gemini Deep Think large language model with a systematic Tree Search (TS) framework and automated numerical feedback, that successfully derived novel, exact analytical solutions for the power spectrum of gravitational radiation emitted by cosmic strings. Specifically, the agent evaluated the core integral $I(N,α)$ for arbitrary loop geometries, directly improving upon recent AI-assisted attempts \cite{BCE+25} that only yielded partial asymptotic solutions. To substantiate our methodological claims regarding AI-accelerated discovery and to ensure transparency, we detail system prompts, search constraints, and intermittent feedback loops that guided the model. The agent identified a suite of 6 different analytical methods, the most elegant of which expands the kernel in Gegenbauer polynomials $C_l^{(3/2)}$ to naturally absorb the integrand's singularities. The methods lead to an asymptotic result for $I(N,α)$ at large $N$ that both agrees with numerical results and also connects to the continuous Feynman parameterization of Quantum Field Theory. We detail both the algorithmic methodology that enabled this discovery and the resulting mathematical derivations.
deferred.why_now: AI-assisted mathematical discovery is a live topic following AlphaGeometry and AlphaProof, and 'AI solved an open physics problem' is a compelling headline. The neuro-symbolic tree-search + LLM feedback loop is a pattern the AI community is actively exploring.
deferred.why_not_higher: The contribution is a specific integral result for cosmic string power spectra — the AI methodology (tree search + LLM numerical feedback) is not novel and the physics application is too narrow to drive a strong general-audience episode. No connection to how AI systems are built, served, or evaluated.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application: result is domain-specific to theoretical physics integrals
Methodology not novel: tree search + LLM feedback is established (AlphaGeometry, AlphaProof pattern)
Zero memory/storage relevance
Does not change how practitioners build or run AI systems
Episode would spend most time explaining cosmic strings, not AI
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: If the paper generalized the neuro-symbolic tree-search framework as a reusable scaffold for open math problems and benchmarked it across multiple domains with clear methodology contributions beyond the physics result.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: An AI agent just cracked an unsolved theoretical physics problem — but is 'LLM plus tree search finds a clever integral trick' actually a new chapter in AI-assisted discovery, or just the latest entry in a long line of narrow wins?

out_of_scope.title: RealWonder: Real-Time Physical Action-Conditioned Video Generation
out_of_scope.abstract: Current video generation models cannot simulate physical consequences of 3D actions like forces and robotic manipulations, as they lack structural understanding of how actions affect 3D scenes. We present RealWonder, the first real-time system for action-conditioned video generation from a single image. Our key insight is using physics simulation as an intermediate bridge: instead of directly encoding continuous actions, we translate them through physics simulation into visual representations (optical flow and RGB) that video models can process. RealWonder integrates three components: 3D reconstruction from single images, physics simulation, and a distilled video generator requiring only 4 diffusion steps. Our system achieves 13.2 FPS at 480x832 resolution, enabling interactive exploration of forces, robot actions, and camera controls on rigid objects, deformable bodies, fluids, and granular materials. We envision RealWonder opens new opportunities to apply video models in immersive experiences, AR/VR, and robot learning. Our code and model weights are publicly available in our project website: https://liuwei283.github.io/RealWonder/
out_of_scope.why_now: Real-time physics-aware video generation from a single image is a technically neat milestone, and the robot learning angle is timely given embodied AI momentum.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a cs.CV/cs.GR systems demo paper — the physics-simulation-as-bridge idea is interesting but doesn't generalize beyond action-conditioned video; neither editorial lens is meaningfully served.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application domain: rigid/deformable/fluid object video synthesis
No memory/storage contribution or signal
sim_negative nearly matches sim_public, indicating topic overlap with out-of-scope content
Method assembles existing components (NeRF-style reconstruction, physics sim, distilled diffusion); no novel AI technique
Episode would be a demo showcase, not an insight episode
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the distillation technique enabling 4-step real-time video generation were shown to transfer to general video or world-model training pipelines.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could poke a photo and watch physics happen in real time — RealWonder wires a physics engine directly into a video diffusion model to make it so.

out_of_scope.title: Locality-Attending Vision Transformer
out_of_scope.abstract: Vision transformers have demonstrated remarkable success in classification by leveraging global self-attention to capture long-range dependencies. However, this same mechanism can obscure fine-grained spatial details crucial for tasks such as segmentation. In this work, we seek to enhance segmentation performance of vision transformers after standard image-level classification training. More specifically, we present a simple yet effective add-on that improves performance on segmentation tasks while retaining vision transformers' image-level recognition capabilities. In our approach, we modulate the self-attention with a learnable Gaussian kernel that biases the attention toward neighboring patches. We further refine the patch representations to learn better embeddings at patch positions. These modifications encourage tokens to focus on local surroundings and ensure meaningful representations at spatial positions, while still preserving the model's ability to incorporate global information. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our modifications, evidenced by substantial segmentation gains on three benchmarks (e.g., over 6% and 4% on ADE20K for ViT Tiny and Base), without changing the training regime or sacrificing classification performance. The code is available at https://github.com/sinahmr/LocAtViT/.
out_of_scope.why_now: Local attention for ViTs is an active area, and the no-retraining angle has practical appeal for segmentation practitioners.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is narrowly scoped to semantic segmentation with no systems, memory, or cross-domain angle; it occupies a crowded literature space.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow segmentation-only validation
Memory/storage connection absent
Incremental over existing local-attention ViT work
No transferability beyond vision tasks demonstrated
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the method generalizes to detection, depth estimation, or video understanding, or demonstrate meaningful inference efficiency gains.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if fixing transformers' spatial blindness for segmentation required nothing more than a Gaussian blur on the attention map?

out_of_scope.title: A Dual-Helix Governance Approach Towards Reliable Agentic AI for WebGIS Development
out_of_scope.abstract: WebGIS development requires rigor, yet agentic AI frequently fails due to five large language model (LLM) limitations: context constraints, cross-session forgetting, stochasticity, instruction failure, and adaptation rigidity. We propose a dual-helix governance framework reframing these challenges as structural governance problems that model capacity alone cannot resolve. We implement the framework as a 3-track architecture (Knowledge, Behavior, Skills) that uses a knowledge graph substrate to stabilize execution by externalizing domain facts and enforcing executable protocols, complemented by a self-learning cycle for autonomous knowledge growth. Applying this to the FutureShorelines WebGIS tool, a governed agent refactored a 2,265-line monolithic codebase into modular ES6 components. Results demonstrated a 51\% reduction in cyclomatic complexity and a 7-point increase in maintainability index. A comparative experiment against a zero-shot LLM confirms that externalized governance, not just model capability, drives operational reliability in geospatial engineering. This approach is implemented in the open-source AgentLoom governance toolkit.
out_of_scope.why_now: Agentic AI reliability is a live topic, and externalizing knowledge via knowledge graphs to stabilize agents is a real pattern. The five LLM failure modes framed as governance problems is a useful lens.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The entire evaluation is a single WebGIS case study with software engineering metrics (cyclomatic complexity, maintainability index), not AI systems metrics; the framework does not transfer to the systems, training, or inference contexts this podcast covers.
downgrade_reasons: Single narrow-domain case study (FutureShorelines WebGIS tool)
Memory/storage connection absent — 'cross-session forgetting' here means knowledge graph persistence, not hardware memory, KV cache, or data movement
Results are software refactoring metrics, not AI workload benchmarks
No evidence the dual-helix framework generalizes beyond geospatial engineering tasks
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate the governance framework on production-scale agentic workflows (e.g., coding agents, multi-agent pipelines) with latency, token, and reliability metrics across diverse domains.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the reason your AI agent keeps failing isn't the model — it's the lack of governance structure around it?

out_of_scope.title: HiMAP-Travel: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Planning for Long-Horizon Constrained Travel
out_of_scope.abstract: Sequential LLM agents fail on long-horizon planning with hard constraints like budgets and diversity requirements. As planning progresses and context grows, these agents drift from global constraints. We propose HiMAP-Travel, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that splits planning into strategic coordination and parallel day-level execution. A Coordinator allocates resources across days, while Day Executors plan independently in parallel. Three key mechanisms enable this: a transactional monitor enforcing budget and uniqueness constraints across parallel agents, a bargaining protocol allowing agents to reject infeasible sub-goals and trigger re-planning, and a single policy trained with GRPO that powers all agents through role conditioning. On TravelPlanner, HiMAP-Travel with Qwen3-8B achieves 52.78% validation and 52.65% test Final Pass Rate (FPR). In a controlled comparison with identical model, training, and tools, it outperforms the sequential DeepTravel baseline by +8.67~pp. It also surpasses ATLAS by +17.65~pp and MTP by +10.0~pp. On FlexTravelBench multi-turn scenarios, it achieves 44.34% (2-turn) and 37.42% (3-turn) FPR while reducing latency 2.5x through parallelization.
out_of_scope.why_now: Hierarchical multi-agent systems with constraint enforcement are an active design space, and the GRPO single-policy-with-role-conditioning approach is a real contribution to agent training. The parallelization latency win is concrete.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper is anchored entirely to travel planning benchmarks and never demonstrates that the transactional monitor or bargaining protocol generalizes beyond itinerary constraints; the method transfer case is unproven.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow domain: results live and die on TravelPlanner / FlexTravelBench
Memory/storage connection is absent — sim_memory score is noise from context-window language
No demonstration of method transfer to other constrained planning domains
Episode would require heavy domain-narrowing to stay technically interesting
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the hierarchical coordinator + transactional monitor working on a second constrained planning domain (e.g., code generation with resource budgets, robotic task scheduling) to establish method generality.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your AI travel agent had a union rep — a bargaining protocol that lets sub-agents reject impossible assignments and force the coordinator to replan on the fly?

out_of_scope.title: Med-V1: Small Language Models for Zero-shot and Scalable Biomedical Evidence Attribution
out_of_scope.abstract: Assessing whether an article supports an assertion is essential for hallucination detection and claim verification. While large language models (LLMs) have the potential to automate this task, achieving strong performance requires frontier models such as GPT-5 that are prohibitively expensive to deploy at scale. To efficiently perform biomedical evidence attribution, we present Med-V1, a family of small language models with only three billion parameters. Trained on high-quality synthetic data newly developed in this study, Med-V1 substantially outperforms (+27.0% to +71.3%) its base models on five biomedical benchmarks unified into a verification format. Despite its smaller size, Med-V1 performs comparably to frontier LLMs such as GPT-5, along with high-quality explanations for its predictions. We use Med-V1 to conduct a first-of-its-kind use case study that quantifies hallucinations in LLM-generated answers under different citation instructions. Results show that the format instruction strongly affects citation validity and hallucination, with GPT-5 generating more claims but exhibiting hallucination rates similar to GPT-4o. Additionally, we present a second use case showing that Med-V1 can automatically identify high-stakes evidence misattributions in clinical practice guidelines, revealing potentially negative public health impacts that are otherwise challenging to identify at scale. Overall, Med-V1 provides an efficient and accurate lightweight alternative to frontier LLMs for practical and real-world applications in biomedical evidence attribution and verification tasks. Med-V1 is available at https://github.com/ncbi-nlp/Med-V1.
out_of_scope.why_now: Medical AI hallucination is a growing concern as LLMs enter clinical workflows; a 3B model matching GPT-5 on biomedical attribution is a notable efficiency result.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper is a domain-specific fine-tuning study — synthetic data distillation onto small models for narrow NLI is well-trodden ground, and nothing here changes how general AI systems are built or served.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow biomedical application with no clear methodological transfer
Synthetic data distillation for domain SLMs is not novel
Zero memory/storage relevance — embedding similarity is keyword noise
Benchmark result, not a systems or architectural contribution
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the synthetic data generation pipeline or attribution rubric were shown to generalize to hallucination detection across domains or at inference scale, it would warrant reconsideration.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: A 3-billion-parameter model trained on synthetic data matches GPT-5 at catching medical hallucinations — but is that a systems win or just domain fine-tuning dressed up?

out_of_scope.title: Embedded Inter-Subject Variability in Adversarial Learning for Inertial Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper addresses the problem of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using data from wearable inertial sensors. An important challenge in HAR is the model's generalization capabilities to new unseen individuals due to inter-subject variability, i.e., the same activity is performed differently by different individuals. To address this problem, we propose a novel deep adversarial framework that integrates the concept of inter-subject variability in the adversarial task, thereby encouraging subject-invariant feature representations and enhancing the classification performance in the HAR problem. Our approach outperforms previous methods in three well-established HAR datasets using a leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation. Further results indicate that our proposed adversarial task effectively reduces inter-subject variability among different users in the feature space, and it outperforms adversarial tasks from previous works when integrated into our framework. Code: https://github.com/FranciscoCalatrava/EmbeddedSubjectVariability.git
out_of_scope.why_now: Adversarial domain adaptation for wearable HAR is an incremental improvement on a decade-old technique (DANN-style invariant features) applied to a narrow embedded-sensor domain.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: Neither editorial lens is served: no memory/storage systems relevance, and the AI audience following foundation models and inference infrastructure has no stake in inertial-sensor activity recognition benchmarks.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow wearable-sensor domain with no generalization to modern AI systems
Memory connection is absent — not proximity to KV cache, scheduling, or data movement
Adversarial domain-invariant feature learning is well-established; contribution is application-specific tuning
Benchmark result on three HAR datasets offers no podcast-worthy narrative for a broad AI audience
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrating the inter-subject adversarial technique improves on-device model adaptation for foundation models or streaming inference across heterogeneous user distributions at scale.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an adversarial trick teach your fitness tracker to recognize how you move — even if you move nothing like the people it trained on?

out_of_scope.title: InstMeter: An Instruction-Level Method to Predict Energy and Latency of DL Model Inference on MCUs
out_of_scope.abstract: Deep learning (DL) models can now run on microcontrollers (MCUs). Through neural architecture search (NAS), we can search DL models that meet the constraints of MCUs. Among various constraints, energy and latency costs of the model inference are critical metrics. To predict them, existing research relies on coarse proxies such as multiply-accumulations (MACs) and model's input parameters, often resulting in inaccurate predictions or requiring extensive data collection. In this paper, we propose InstMeter, a predictor leveraging MCUs' clock cycles to accurately estimate the energy and latency of DL models. Clock cycles are fundamental metrics reflecting MCU operations, directly determining energy and latency costs. Furthermore, a unique property of our predictor is its strong linearity, allowing it to be simple and accurate. We thoroughly evaluate InstMeter under different scenarios, MCUs, and software settings. Compared with state-of-the-art studies, InstMeter can reduce the energy and latency prediction errors by $3\times$ and $6.5\times$, respectively, while requiring $100\times$ and $10\times$ less training data. In the NAS scenario, InstMeter can fully exploit the energy budget, identifying optimal DL models with higher inference accuracy. We also evaluate InstMeter's generalization performance through various experiments on three ARM MCUs (Cortex-M4, M7, M33) and one RISC-V-based MCU (ESP32-C3), different compilation options (-Os, -O2), GCC versions (v7.3, v10.3), application scenarios (keyword spotting, image recognition), dynamic voltage and frequency scaling, temperatures (21°C, 43°C), and software settings (TFLMv2.4, TFLMvCI). We will open our source codes and the MCU-specific benchmark datasets.
out_of_scope.why_now: TinyML on MCUs is a real deployment problem, and clock-cycle-based cost prediction for NAS is a credible improvement over MAC-counting proxies.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The audience is narrow TinyML practitioners on Cortex-M/RISC-V silicon — this doesn't transfer to LLM serving, KV cache, or any of the systems problems the podcast covers.
downgrade_reasons: MCU-specific scope (Cortex-M4/M7/M33, ESP32-C3) — not modern AI infrastructure
Memory/storage connection absent — no bandwidth, offload, tiering, or cache angle
Benchmark result over a niche workload (keyword spotting, image recognition on MCUs)
No transferability to large-model training or serving contexts
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the clock-cycle prediction method generalized to GPU/NPU kernel-level latency modeling for LLM inference scheduling, it would be relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could predict exactly how much battery your tiny AI model burns before you even run NAS — and it only needed 1% of the training data everyone else uses?

out_of_scope.title: ConTSG-Bench: A Unified Benchmark for Conditional Time Series Generation
out_of_scope.abstract: Conditional time series generation plays a critical role in addressing data scarcity and enabling causal analysis in real-world applications. Despite its increasing importance, the field lacks a standardized and systematic benchmarking framework for evaluating generative models across diverse conditions. To address this gap, we introduce the Conditional Time Series Generation Benchmark (ConTSG-Bench). ConTSG-Bench comprises a large-scale, well-aligned dataset spanning diverse conditioning modalities and levels of semantic abstraction, first enabling systematic evaluation of representative generation methods across these dimensions with a comprehensive suite of metrics for generation fidelity and condition adherence. Both the quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behaviors have revealed the traits and limitations of the current approaches, highlighting critical challenges and promising research directions, particularly with respect to precise structural controllability and downstream task utility under complex conditions.
out_of_scope.why_now: Time series generation benchmarks address real data scarcity problems in finance, healthcare, and IoT, but this is a domain-specific contribution without broad AI audience pull.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: Conditional time series generation is a narrow application niche; the benchmark does not generalize to the foundation models, systems, or memory topics that define this podcast's two lenses.
downgrade_reasons: No memory/storage connection — the 0.264 memory score is embedding noise
Narrow task domain (time series) with limited transferability to LLM or systems work
Benchmark-only contribution; no new method or architectural insight
Does not change how modern AI systems are built, trained, or served
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the benchmark exposed scaling laws or generation fidelity failures tied to transformer KV cache behavior or large-context time series modeling, it would become relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: We finally have a standardized test for AI that generates fake data — but the field it's testing is too niche to matter to most listeners.

out_of_scope.title: AILS-NTUA at SemEval-2026 Task 10: Agentic LLMs for Psycholinguistic Marker Extraction and Conspiracy Endorsement Detection
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper presents a novel agentic LLM pipeline for SemEval-2026 Task 10 that jointly extracts psycholinguistic conspiracy markers and detects conspiracy endorsement. Unlike traditional classifiers that conflate semantic reasoning with structural localization, our decoupled design isolates these challenges. For marker extraction, we propose Dynamic Discriminative Chain-of-Thought (DD-CoT) with deterministic anchoring to resolve semantic ambiguity and character-level brittleness. For conspiracy detection, an "Anti-Echo Chamber" architecture, consisting of an adversarial Parallel Council adjudicated by a Calibrated Judge, overcomes the "Reporter Trap," where models falsely penalize objective reporting. Achieving 0.24 Macro F1 (+100\% over baseline) on S1 and 0.79 Macro F1 (+49\%) on S2, with the S1 system ranking 3rd on the development leaderboard, our approach establishes a versatile paradigm for interpretable, psycholinguistically-grounded NLP.
out_of_scope.why_now: The adversarial multi-agent 'council + judge' architecture is a live design pattern, and SemEval-2026 timing gives it immediate relevance. But the application domain—psycholinguistic conspiracy marker extraction—is too narrow for this podcast's audience.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a shared-task system paper; the methodology is evaluated only on one niche NLP benchmark with no evidence of transfer to the general AI systems problems this podcast covers.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow SemEval task paper, not a general method contribution
Zero memory/storage connection
Results are task-specific; no cross-domain validation
Techniques (DD-CoT, Anti-Echo Chamber) described but not ablated for generality
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrating the adversarial council/calibrated-judge architecture improves LLM reasoning or reduces hallucination across diverse general-purpose tasks.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you gave an AI a devil's advocate and a judge to stop it from falsely punishing journalists for reporting on conspiracy theories—and it actually worked?

out_of_scope.title: AI-Assisted Moot Courts: Simulating Justice-Specific Questioning in Oral Arguments
out_of_scope.abstract: In oral arguments, judges probe attorneys with questions about the factual record, legal claims, and the strength of their arguments. To prepare for this questioning, both law schools and practicing attorneys rely on moot courts: practice simulations of appellate hearings. Leveraging a dataset of U.S. Supreme Court oral argument transcripts, we examine whether AI models can effectively simulate justice-specific questioning for moot court-style training. Evaluating oral argument simulation is challenging because there is no single correct question for any given turn. Instead, effective questioning should reflect a combination of desirable qualities, such as anticipating substantive legal issues, detecting logical weaknesses, and maintaining an appropriately adversarial tone. We introduce a two-layer evaluation framework that assesses both the realism and pedagogical usefulness of simulated questions using complementary proxy metrics. We construct and evaluate both prompt-based and agentic oral argument simulators. We find that simulated questions are often perceived as realistic by human annotators and achieve high recall of ground truth substantive legal issues. However, models still face substantial shortcomings, including low diversity in question types and sycophancy. Importantly, these shortcomings would remain undetected under naive evaluation approaches.
out_of_scope.why_now: AI simulation of domain-expert behavior is active, and the sycophancy finding echoes broader LLM alignment concerns. The two-layer evaluation framing is a minor methodological contribution.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow legal application paper — Supreme Court oral argument simulation — with no connection to the podcast's two editorial lenses and limited transfer to how AI systems are built or deployed.
downgrade_reasons: Legal domain application, minimal transfer to AI systems or infrastructure
Memory/storage connection is absent
Evaluation framework is domain-specific, not reusable beyond legal simulation
Core findings (sycophancy, low diversity) are already well-known LLM failure modes
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the evaluation framework generalized to multi-turn LLM simulation benchmarks across domains and exposed new systemic reasoning gaps.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can AI argue like a Supreme Court justice — and why does it keep agreeing with you when it should push back?

out_of_scope.title: RoboPocket: Improve Robot Policies Instantly with Your Phone
out_of_scope.abstract: Scaling imitation learning is fundamentally constrained by the efficiency of data collection. While handheld interfaces have emerged as a scalable solution for in-the-wild data acquisition, they predominantly operate in an open-loop manner: operators blindly collect demonstrations without knowing the underlying policy's weaknesses, leading to inefficient coverage of critical state distributions. Conversely, interactive methods like DAgger effectively address covariate shift but rely on physical robot execution, which is costly and difficult to scale. To reconcile this trade-off, we introduce RoboPocket, a portable system that enables Robot-Free Instant Policy Iteration using single consumer smartphones. Its core innovation is a Remote Inference framework that visualizes the policy's predicted trajectory via Augmented Reality (AR) Visual Foresight. This immersive feedback allows collectors to proactively identify potential failures and focus data collection on the policy's weak regions without requiring a physical robot. Furthermore, we implement an asynchronous Online Finetuning pipeline that continuously updates the policy with incoming data, effectively closing the learning loop in minutes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboPocket adheres to data scaling laws and doubles the data efficiency compared to offline scaling strategies, overcoming their long-standing efficiency bottleneck. Moreover, our instant iteration loop also boosts sample efficiency by up to 2$\times$ in distributed environments a small number of interactive corrections per person. Project page and videos: https://robo-pocket.github.io.
out_of_scope.why_now: Smartphone-based AR teleoperation for robot data collection is a practical engineering contribution, but it solves a robotics-domain logistics problem rather than advancing general AI methodology.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper is a systems contribution specific to robot imitation learning data pipelines; the method does not transfer to language, vision, or general ML contexts covered by this podcast.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow robotics application with no clear transfer to general AI systems
No memory/storage connection — embedding similarity was a false positive
Engineering contribution (smartphone AR teleoperation), not a methodology advance
Episode audience would be robotics practitioners, not a broad AI audience
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the AR Visual Foresight or online finetuning loop were shown to generalize beyond robot manipulation — e.g., to RLHF data collection or active learning at scale — it would warrant reconsideration.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could debug a robot's blind spots from your couch using only your phone and augmented reality?

out_of_scope.title: Stan: An LLM-based thermodynamics course assistant
out_of_scope.abstract: Discussions of AI in education focus predominantly on student-facing tools -- chatbots, tutors, and problem generators -- while the potential for the same infrastructure to support instructors remains largely unexplored. We describe Stan, a suite of tools for an undergraduate chemical engineering thermodynamics course built on a data pipeline that we develop and deploy in dual roles: serving students and supporting instructors from a shared foundation of lecture transcripts and a structured textbook index. On the student side, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline answers natural-language queries by extracting technical terms, matching them against the textbook index, and synthesizing grounded responses with specific chapter and page references. On the instructor side, the same transcript corpus is processed through structured analysis pipelines that produce per-lecture summaries, identify student questions and moments of confusion, and catalog the anecdotes and analogies used to motivate difficult material -- providing a searchable, semester-scale record of teaching that supports course reflection, reminders, and improvement. All components, including speech-to-text transcription, structured content extraction, and interactive query answering, run entirely on locally controlled hardware using open-weight models (Whisper large-v3, Llama~3.1 8B) with no dependence on cloud APIs, ensuring predictable costs, full data privacy, and reproducibility independent of third-party services. We describe the design, implementation, and practical failure modes encountered when deploying 7--8 billion parameter models for structured extraction over long lecture transcripts, including context truncation, bimodal output distributions, and schema drift, along with the mitigations that resolved them.
out_of_scope.why_now: Local LLM deployment for education is an active area, and the documented failure modes (context truncation, bimodal outputs, schema drift) with 7-8B models have mild practical value for RAG practitioners.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a single-course deployment paper in chemical engineering; the methods (RAG, Whisper, Llama) are all off-the-shelf and the contribution is operational, not architectural or broadly transferable.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow domain application (one thermodynamics course)
No novel method or system contribution
Memory/storage lens absent — local hardware mention is incidental
Does not change how AI systems are built, trained, or served
Episode hook is essentially 'professor builds RAG chatbot'
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the paper generalized the failure-mode mitigations into a reusable framework for structured extraction with small models, it would become relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: A chemical engineering professor wires Whisper and Llama into a RAG system to help students and grade his own lectures — and documents every way small models fell apart along the way.

out_of_scope.title: An Exploration-Analysis-Disambiguation Reasoning Framework for Word Sense Disambiguation with Low-Parameter LLMs
out_of_scope.abstract: Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) remains a key challenge in Natural Language Processing (NLP), especially when dealing with rare or domain-specific senses that are often misinterpreted. While modern high-parameter Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4-Turbo have shown state-of-the-art WSD performance, their computational and energy demands limit scalability. This study investigates whether low-parameter LLMs (<4B parameters) can achieve comparable results through fine-tuning strategies that emphasize reasoning-driven sense identification. Using the FEWS dataset augmented with semi-automated, rationale-rich annotations, we fine-tune eight small-scale open-source LLMs (e.g. Gemma and Qwen). Our results reveal that Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-based reasoning combined with neighbour-word analysis achieves performance comparable to GPT-4-Turbo in zero-shot settings. Importantly, Gemma-3-4B and Qwen-3-4B models consistently outperform all medium-parameter baselines and state-of-the-art models on FEWS, with robust generalization to unseen senses. Furthermore, evaluation on the unseen "Fool Me If You Can'' dataset confirms strong cross-domain adaptability without task-specific fine-tuning. This work demonstrates that with carefully crafted reasoning-centric fine-tuning, low-parameter LLMs can deliver accurate WSD while substantially reducing computational and energy demands.
out_of_scope.why_now: Small models matching GPT-4-scale performance via fine-tuning is a live topic, and efficiency gains matter broadly. However, WSD is a decades-old NLP subtask far removed from current AI practitioner concerns.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow benchmark paper on a specific linguistic task; the CoT fine-tuning approach is well-established and the WSD-specific findings do not transfer to the systems or architecture questions this podcast covers.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow task paper: WSD is not at the frontier of AI practice in 2026
Memory/storage connection is absent — 'low-parameter' is about model size, not memory systems
CoT fine-tuning is not a novel method; contribution is task-specific
Benchmark results on FEWS do not generalize to broader AI workflows
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the reasoning framework demonstrated clear transfer to diverse language understanding tasks and showed systems-level inference efficiency gains at scale, it would merit reconsideration.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a 4B-parameter model beat GPT-4 at understanding word meaning — and what does that tell us about how reasoning, not raw scale, drives language intelligence?

out_of_scope.title: HDLFORGE: A Two-Stage Multi-Agent Framework for Efficient Verilog Code Generation with Adaptive Model Escalation
out_of_scope.abstract: We present HDLFORGE, a two-stage multi-agent framework for automated Verilog generation that optimizes the trade-off between generation speed and accuracy. The system uses a compact coder with a medium-sized LLM by default (Stage A) and escalates to a stronger coder with an ultra-large LLM (Stage B) only when needed, guided by a calibrated score from inexpensive diagnostics including compilation, lint, and smoke tests. A key innovation is a counterexample-guided formal agent that converts bounded-model-checking traces into reusable micro-tests, significantly reducing bug detection time and repair iterations. The portable escalation controller can wrap existing Verilog LLM pipelines without modifying their internals. Evaluated on VerilogEval Human, VerilogEval V2, and RTLLM benchmarks, HDLFORGE demonstrates improved accuracy-latency trade-offs compared to single-stage systems through comprehensive analysis of wall-clock time distributions, escalation thresholds, and agent ablations. On VerilogEval Human and VerilogEval V2, HDLFORGE-Qwen achieves 91.2% and 91.8% Pass@1 with roughly 50% lower median latency, dramatically improving accuracy over other medium-sized models, and 97.2% Pass@5 on RTLLM.
out_of_scope.why_now: LLM-assisted hardware design (EDA) is an active niche, and adaptive model escalation with formal verification feedback is a real engineering contribution for chip designers.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The audience is EDA/RTL engineers, not the broad AI community; the method does not feed back into how AI systems are built, trained, or served.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application domain: Verilog/HDL code generation for chip design
Memory/storage connection absent — 'Hardware' category refers to EDA, not AI memory systems
Transferability limited: formal counterexample agent is tightly coupled to Verilog semantics
No relevance to LLM serving, training infrastructure, or AI systems at large
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the adaptive escalation controller were validated on general software code generation or integrated into an AI chip design loop that demonstrably reduces inference hardware costs.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can LLMs write the silicon that future AI runs on — and does HDLFORGE's two-stage escalation make that practical?

out_of_scope.title: MOOSEnger -- a Domain-Specific AI Agent for the MOOSE Ecosystem
out_of_scope.abstract: MOOSEnger is a tool-enabled AI agent tailored to the Multiphysics Object-Oriented Simulation Environment (MOOSE). MOOSE cases are specified in HIT ".i" input files; the large object catalog and strict syntax make initial setup and debugging slow. MOOSEnger offers a conversational workflow that turns natural-language intent into runnable inputs by combining retrieval-augmented generation over curated docs/examples with deterministic, MOOSE-aware parsing, validation, and execution tools. A core-plus-domain architecture separates reusable agent infrastructure (configuration, registries, tool dispatch, retrieval services, persistence, and evaluation) from a MOOSE plugin that adds HIT-based parsing, syntax-preserving ingestion of input files, and domain-specific utilities for input repair and checking. An input precheck pipeline removes hidden formatting artifacts, fixes malformed HIT structure with a bounded grammar-constrained loop, and resolves invalid object types via similarity search over an application syntax registry. Inputs are then validated and optionally smoke-tested with the MOOSE runtime in the loop via an MCP-backed execution backend (with local fallback), translating solver diagnostics into iterative verify-and-correct updates. Built-in evaluation reports RAG metrics (faithfulness, relevancy, context precision/recall) and end-to-end success by actual execution. On a 125-prompt benchmark spanning diffusion, transient heat conduction, solid mechanics, porous flow, and incompressible Navier--Stokes, MOOSEnger achieves a 0.93 execution pass rate versus 0.08 for an LLM-only baseline.
out_of_scope.why_now: Domain-specific agents for scientific computing are an active area, and the 0.93 vs 0.08 execution pass rate is a striking result that demonstrates RAG + tool-use closing a real usability gap.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: MOOSE is a niche multiphysics simulation framework used in nuclear and mechanical engineering — the audience overlap with AI podcast listeners is minimal, and the architecture (RAG + grammar-constrained repair + execution loop) applies existing patterns without generalizing them.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow domain: MOOSE ecosystem only, no transfer to mainstream AI workflows
